comments.title: Rune Poems, Welsh Translation, and the Mabinogion
comments.content:
Good evening and happy Belteinne! For anyone who doesn't celebrate Belteinne, a happy beginning of May or simply a happy Sunday evening/Monday morning to you.

First, something to share for the commentariat: The Rune Poems, edited by P.D. Brown and Michael Moynihan: https://arcanaeuropamedia.com/products/the-rune-poems . The main primary sources on the various versions of the Runes are the Rune Poems. This book contains a new translation of each of the major Rune Poems. Additionally, and perhaps more excitingly, it also contains a number of poems about the Runes by current esoteric practitioners interested in the Runes. If you're at all interested in the Runes, it's well worth checking out.

As for questions, I have three, all related to reading Welsh for esoteric wisdom:

1) I have not been able to find any discussion of the meaning/etymology of Einigan/Einigain/Einigair/Einiger, other than that "ein" is the (modern?) Welsh word for "our", and that "Einigen" is (coincidentally?) German for "unite". Any pointers on where I might track this down?

2) More broadly, any recommendations on a Welsh Dictionary for supplementing study of the Mabinogion?

3) Building on the above, any chance that a version of the Mabinogion with footnotes and/or esoteric commentary is one of your very many irons in the fire? Or is The Dolmen Arch as close as we're getting there?

Thanks as always for hosting this space and your time and attention in responding.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Rune Poems, Welsh Translation, and the Mabinogion
replies.content: Somewhere JMG wrote that the myth of Einigen might be quite old; the name Einigen might come from an ancient Celtic Oinogenos, which would mean "the one-born"  or something similar. The similarity with the German verb "einigen" is probably entirely coincidental.

replies.title: Re: Rune Poems, Welsh Translation, and the Mabinogion
replies.content:
Thanks for the rune poem material!

1) French Druids have insisted for a while now that it's descended from the Gaulish name *Oinogenos, "only-born." I have no idea if they're right or not, but I cover that and several other details from the Einigan story in an article in Trilithon:

http://aoda.org/publications/trilithon-the-journal-of-the-aoda/trilithon-ordering-information/

2) You need Middle Weish, not modern Welsh. There's a fine free course online here:

https://www.mit.edu/~dfm/canol/

Dictionaries of Middle Welsh are pricey; I've made use of online dictionaries for that reason.

2) It's not something I have in process, certainly.

replies.title: Re: Rune Poems, Welsh Translation, and the Mabinogion
replies.content: The problem with the aforementioned etymology of Einigen is that the word for 'one' in Welsh, derived from the Celtic root *oino-, is 'un', not 'ein'.

replies.title: Re: Rune Poems, Welsh Translation, and the Mabinogion
replies.content: Thanks very much! Interestingly, I arrived too late to meet Dr. Morgan, but I took classes with many of his colleagues and heard the name quite a bit in the Classics department of the University of Texas. Synchronicitous!

replies.title: Gareth Morgan's Language Teaching Skill Applied to Greek
replies.content:
I was looking at Dr. Morgan's bio a bit more and I was embarrassed to be reminded why his name sounded so familiar: he designed the intensive Summer Greek program that I took in college. Given the interest in Plato and other things Greek here, I thought some folks might be interested to see that the approach from that program has been adapted into a book, with a supplementary website: https://jamesfpatterson.com/gml/

It really is a fantastic way to learn a language for reading - it focuses on the roots of the words and their morphologies rather than beginning with rote memorization of lots of vocabulary. As a personal testimonial, I went from unable to even sound out the Greek alphabet to reading Plato's apology in a summer. Now, don't get me wrong - it was an extremely busy summer, but also enjoyable, if you like that sort of thing.

replies.title: Re: Gareth Morgan's Language Teaching Skill Applied to Greek
replies.content: Hmm! Thanks for this.

replies.title: Re: Rune Poems, Welsh Translation, and the Mabinogion
replies.content: In German, adj einiger/einige/einiges means a few/some and adj einig means united/in agreement, from MHG einec/einic, OHG einac = only, alone (according to my Duden German dictionary)

comments.title: 3 Questions
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,

Blessings to you and my fellow commentators.

1. I’m currently studying the Tree of Life. One night, after reading about Binah, Dante’s Paradiso popped into my mind. It was as if someone was telling me to research on this. When I did, my mind was blown. Dante’s journey through the spheres (from the Moon to the Primum Mobile and beyond) in heaven is basically the reverse Flaming Sword. Even God’s appearance as “three equally large circles” in the Empyrean reminds me of the Three Veils of Negative Existence. Do you think Dante knew of the Kabbalah and/or was an initiate of a mystery tradition?
2. Saturn is the only planet associated with a Sephirot above the Abyss, in the Supernal Triad. Based on this, can one assume that Saturn has a power and influence beyond the other planets?
3. I’m traveling out of the city this week to an island that become a fairly popular tourist destination over the past decade (I live in Asia, btw). I plan to continue doing my daily practices (LBRP, MP, Circulation) while I am there. Do I need to inform the ‘spirits of the land’ that I will be performing my daily practices? If so, what is the proper way to do so?

Thank you for hosting Magic Mondays.

replies.title: Re: 3 Questions
replies.content:
1) In the Middle Ages, the vision of reality that we now consider espteric wisdom was the ordinary worldview of most educated Europeans. Dante may have had access to a mystery tradition, but he might also have picked up a lot of the Neoplatonism that was the foundation of ordinary thought in his time.

2) Depends on your version of the Tree! In the version I currently use, Uranus corresponds to Chokmah and Neptune to Kether. In any case, Saturn traditionally has certain spiritual dimensions not present in the other planets, but it's not stronger than they are.

3) Only if you're doing them outdoors in an area with plants. If you're indoors, it'll just be another of those funny things that humans do.

replies.title: Re: 3 Questions
replies.content:
I strongly suspect La Divina Commedia can be used as an initiatory process; David Pantano has touched briefly on this in his book The Magic Door on Italic mystery currents. In my view, the spheres of Paradiso correspond with the sefirot: note the attributes and behavior of the happy spirits in the heavens, among other things.

Mark Mirsky has speculated that Dante was familiar with the Kabbalah (Dante, Eros, and Kabbalah), but his treatment deals more with erotic undercurrents he traces in the work, although he does find some suggestive historical connections. Both Guenon and Reghini dealt with the Commedia as an esoteric work, also. Here's a post of mine with some references.

comments.title: Sun Music
comments.content:
Hi JMG and possibly commentariat. I've started doing a daily planetary prayer practice, and I like to play music from Gustav Holst's The Planets. The only problem is Holst never composed movements for the Sun or Moon. The Moon is easy, an all-strings version of Claire de Lune and the first movememt of Moonlight Sonata work great. But I don't have anything better than the Beatles' Here Comes the Sun for the Sun's music (it lightens the heart and feels as cheery as a dose of St. John's Wort, but it's not very regal or awe-inspiring).

Do you or anybody else have any suggestions for some very solar classical/orchestral music?

replies.title: Re: Sun Music
replies.content: The piece "Hail the Conquering Hero Comes" from Handel's Judas Maccabeus, or the prelude to act 2 of Mozart's The Magic Flute (which always makes me think of sunrise), might be worth considering.

replies.title: Re: Sun Music
replies.content:
Consider that most of the old-school Christian hymns are designed with the intention of being sung or played on a Sunday morning, and many of them are quite regal in tone.

Beethoven's "Ode to Joy" in particular strikes me as quite solar both in its musical structure and in its lyrics (especially the line "Fill us with the light of day".

replies.title: Re: Sun Music
replies.content:
I also play Gustav Holst in my daily planetary practice.
For the Moon besides Clair de Lune I play instrumental versions of "Blue Moon"and Sting's "Sister Moon" or "Moon Over Bourbon Street".
For the Sun I play a tune I found on Youtube's channel "Electronic Gems". Check it out and see if it suits your taste:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oxoqm05c7yA

replies.title: Re: Sun Music
replies.content:
The intro to Also Sprach Zarathustra by Strauss might be a good solar piece. It always makes my hair stand on end when I listen to it.

JLfromNH/Sepia Jocular Griffin

replies.title: Re: Sun Music
replies.content:
You may also want to consider "Morning Mood" from Grieg's Peer Gynt suite: while the first iteration of the theme is gentle (like just before dawn), its later iterations are more powerful and regal (with the full orchestra playing, like the sun rising above the horizon and shining in his full glory). If it is not bombastic enough for your taste, try Aaron Copland's "Fanfare for the Common Man": it has always had a solar feeling to me (I used to perform it as part of a brass quintet with tympani - it would literally make me high). YMMV.

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Sun Music
replies.content: Perhaps your solar music could be silence. When I am outside under the stars, nature serenades me with many sounds. When I am outside under the bright midday, midsummer sun, it is often quiet as all creatures hide and rest in the shade. I also associate the sun with life, and in silence I can hear solar life moving through me - my heartbeat and breathing. Silence could also serve as a balancing force to the other days of music.

replies.title: Re: Sun Music
replies.content:
Another suggestion: In the last scene of Wagner's Siegfried, when the hero awakens the sleeping Valkyrie, she sings (with long pauses filled with exalted orchestral music):

Heil dir, Sonne!
Heil dir, Licht!
Heil dir, leuchtender Tag!

Gray Hat

replies.title: Re: Sun Music - Strahlen, Rays
replies.content:
You could check out Strahlen by Karlheinz Stockhausen. It's an excerpted piece from Sontag aus Licht, or Sunday from Light. Licht is his cycle of operas, one for each day of the week. As such, each opera for each day of the week contains many correspondences relating to that weekday/planet. Furthermore, due to the modular nature of the opera, each day also contains within it a number of works that can be extracted and played on their own and/or variations from within that day.

Strahlen comes from Sunday, and translated from German means Rays. Written for for a percussionist (vibraphone and/or glockenspiel) and ten-channel electronic music sound recording. Absorb the Rays!

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Fp4rHzGrZao

"The work's title suggests an impression of overwhelming radiance. The central idea of the composition is the simultaneous layering of five different tempos. These are chosen in various combinations from a scale of seven metronomic tempos: 30, 40, 53.5, 71, 95, 134, and 180 per minute."

It's a beautiful and rather accesible piece of his work...open the door to the sun, and go down the rabbit hole!

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: Re: Sun Music
replies.content: If you enjoy Wagner, "Hail dir, Sonne! Hail dir, Licht!" from Siegfried is when Siegfried awakens Brunnhilde from her magic sleep, and she hails him/the Sun/the light, so it is at least solar meaning.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

what is the best way to explain / say something about occult practice to someone not familiar with it, if that subject comes up for any reason?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good question. As I have Aspergers syndrome I'm not the person to ask about how to deal gracefully with other people! Anyone else?

replies.title: Two strategies, short and compasionate
replies.content:
The 3 strategies I use are:
- silence
- deflection
- concealment

Seriously. I can sympathise with anyone who would wish to have someone to talk to about the occult. But as much as I wish to have such people in my life, the reality is that the vast majority is frightened by such topics, and the rest have wild preconceptions.
So I just keep quiet. Nothing to see here.
If the topic does come up, maybe someone mentions divination, God is a fairly common topic, or witches. I either deflect with a joke or some double meaning comment.
If the person involved shows some actual curiosity I tend to go along, but I mask it in philosophical, psychological or christian terms.
I have yet to encounter someone who understood the double meaning behind the expressions I use.

jmg. Is silence the best option. Hard discipline as it is, it does provide relative comfort for a mage in social circumstances. But on the other hand, would it not be better to speak out and dispel those preconceived notions and maybe make others interested?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I like to start off where the person I'm taking to is already comfortable.

One way to give someone the basics if they're just asking what you're doing is that it's a mind training system with extra old philosophy attached.

Another way could be to point out that if a concept is shared among all human langues then it's probably universally experienced as a reality. Things such as love, or the colour blue. The fact that there's a word for life energy in basically every langue but many missing the colour blue indicates it's something that's commonly experienced. (https://www.businessinsider.com/your-language-determines-how-you-see-colors-2018-4)

What kind of situation are you trying to navigate? How much are you trying to explain, to who and how open are they to this general area.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The question which I posed was a hypothetical one, in case the subject of occultism comes up.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Occultism is pretty broad. What about easing into it with things that have crossed over to mainstream territory like astrology, herbs as medicine, or healing with crystals? Someone who is hostile or negative about these things is not likely to appreciate anything deeper. But you're not taking a big risk by wondering out loud if there's more to astrology than sun signs. Or asking someone if they think crystals can help people. Someone who expresses enthusiasm or sincere interest might be worth a cautious poke.

Psychedelic jocular puca

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Depends what that 'something' you are trying to say is. One tip is to not use the word 'occult' as it can have negative connotations in the mainstream. In my experience, people tend to respond more positively to terms like my 'spiritual practice'.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: +1 on this. I usually just mention (if I feel they even need to know) "my meditation practice"

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
While some might consider that an inaccurate or incomplete description, I console myself with the fact that the word "meditation" quite often connotes nothing other than some vaguely spiritual...thing...to a lot of Westerners, which of course is exactly how
much they need to know in virtually every case.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I do this too. I think it's really not worth using the term "occultism" in the presence of those who have not yet showed open-mindedness to spirituality-related things. There plenty of innocent-sounding substitutes and euphemisms that can be used in its place to casually describes one's interests. Philosophy, metaphysics, spirituality, "meditation practices," ect. If the person I'm conversing with shows no interest in these things then I see no reason to carry on the conversation, much less escalate it to words and concepts that often creep out or confuse the normies.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Keep it simple.  Have a basic definition of what the occult is, what magic is, etc., and offer these.  If the person is interested, and respectful, you can dive in deeper after that.  In my experience, most people either smirk or just change the subject.  In those cases, it is not worth your time to keep the discussion going.  My best friend is a materialist atheist, and the biggest wastes of my time have been trying to get him to even see the occult or theism as a possibly valid alternative to his point of view.

replies.title: How deep
replies.content:
Usually people/ friends around me know, that I'm into something concerning spirituality. Only a very few know that I started to go into "deeper waters", so usually it's me wanting to share something I learned here. If that happens I Start with: you know that I'm kind of a hippie? (in German I say: " Spiri-Tante" -spiri-aunt). Then I say that I read this blog, from this American Archdruid, which I find useful. Depending on the reaction to that info I either go deeper or stay at the surface. A very materialistic minded friend of mine got the surface answer and because of TADR refers to JMG as The "oil-loony" (sorry!!) but I still can drop information starting with: the oil-loony has written....and his subconscious can work with that information, even if the conscious mind labels it as loony.

Or maybe put differentely: in the Weird of Hali Serie JMG has (to my mind) done a great job to make tentacles, gills and pseudopods like- and lovable with getting more and more direct as the story continued and I think that can be copied.

Probably peppered with good metaphors.

I have a daily prayer and my little kid wanted to know that I was doing, and I said: I'm saying "Hello", to that power.

Hm, long answer, was that even your question?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I would focus on the importance of consciousness in your practice. This fits in nicely with Dion Fortune’s definition of magic.

Also, build up the skills of acceptance in yourself that the other person you’re talking to will not get it. And it’s ok, it says nothing negative about yourself or the other person. Think about your own journey to understand whatever concept you’re thinking of referencing in a conversation. If you feel you have nudged the person to any degree you’ve accomplished something magnificent.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG & Commentariat,

How long do I leave a jar of magical stuff buried by the side of the road to dissipate its energies? If it surfaces again, do I re-bury it, or can I take it out with the trash after a while?

In mid-Fall last year, I found a glass jar that had rolled down the hill from my neighbour's yard. Not too surprising, as they both have the look and feel of dabblers in the occult. It had some brown solids in it, some liquids and maybe sticks and other things. I took it for some sort of magical working, and was advised here to bury it un-opened by the side of the road so that whatever energies were inside it could dissipate with the passing traffic.

I buried it on the edge of my property line in the middle of the night without lights, and pulled a flap of turf aside, replacing it so as to hide the hole. I am pretty sure I was not observed.

After I buried the jar, one of my neighbours began to walk around the road at the edge of my property. Maybe she could feel that she had lost something, I'm not sure.

Four months later, the neighbours moved away.
Five months later, the jar top poked up out of the earth.

I re-buried the jar--but could I just throw it away at this point? How long do I need to leave it buried by the side of the road?

Thank you for hosting this forum, and thanks to all who comment! I have learned a great deal here.
--Obsidian Paradoxical Neanderthal

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Bury it again, in a place where it won't come to the surface. It doesn't have to be by the side of the road but it shouldn't be where anyone can get at it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
The moment, as in the exact day, the Sun entered Aries and left Pisces my life went ablaze into minor chaos. It's not the first time I've noticed this and I already have a set of things and moods I do and experience that seem to correlate to astrological phenomena.

What astrological concepts and/or techniques do I need to learn to figure out what's going on and do something about it? I know that there is a way to take the current chart and cross reference it with my natal chart but I don't know the details.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
What you're asking about is the interpretation of transits. When a planet in the heavens right now aspects the position a planet had in your natal chart, that's a transit. There are books that reference it, mostly in terms of the slow outer planets; I've got a book in process that covers transits to the natal chart as a means of timing, which may help when it's available.

Basically what you do is look at the aspect and interpret it as though it's temporarily part of your natal chart. If (let's say) the transiting Sun squares your natal Mars, yeah, you can expect some chaos!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks!

So you take a planet, say Mars, and ignore the natal Mars and use the current one to see the new aspects it makes to the other planets? Is this a process that is done for all the planets one at a time?

Have you found a particular orb size useful for transits?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's a little subtler than that. Both your natal Mars and the Mars in the sky are both relevant, because the Mars in the sky can aspect your natal Mars! Transiting Mars trine natal Mars, for example, gives you strength of will, vitality, and energy; transiting Mars square natal Mars makes you irritable and prone to angry outbursts, and so on. You track all the planets and see which of them are aspecting your natal planets at any given time, and that gives you a sense of what's happening in your life and psyche just then.

I give transits 3° applying and 1° separating.

replies.title: Re: Break in the life line
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, it's a common misconception that breaks in the life line indicate death. They don't. They indicate that your vitality comes in fits and starts, and that you need to take more time to rest and think things over than most people -- very often, more time than you give yourself. Breaks in both life lines can mean a serious illness, though it may be one you've already had -- the life line begins between the thumb and index finger and ends near the wrist, so you can divide that distance into an average lifespan and see where in your life the breaks happen. comments.title: Long Dark Tea-Time of the Soul

Happy Belteinne!

I was gifted a dark night of the soul last week, in which I saw clearly how I had caused my own suffering in regards to my current health issue. Part of it was an honest mistake, but the other part was utterly foolish. It hadn't occurred to me that almost immediately after surgery from a different injury, I would need to go back to the doctor and start the process all over with a new injury. That, and I hide my pain, so my husband didn't realize he needed to urge me to go to the doctor. It took being in pain for 2.5 years to finally get an X-ray and realize I'm injured.

This frustration opened the door to a deep anger, where I mentally was violently destructive in imagining revenge toward myself for causing my own suffering. This only lasted a few moments and must have ended due to an angel's prompting: my husband came into the next room and declared a Lord of the Rings watch party.

The next day I realized this was the same pattern with my Devouring Mother Archetype growing up. Much of it was an honest mistake; as a child I didn't realize my mother could twist me mentally and emotionally, and that such things were abuse. It wasn't until she tried to physically attack me at age 30 that I finally processed this was abuse.

The same dark pattern of self-hatred flows all through that. I hated myself for letting myself be twisted by her, for never standing up. Though the one time I did was at age 30 when she then physically attacked me, so standing up as a child would have been a bad idea.

It feels like I opened up this deep, terrible thing in the ground and finally let it dissipate into the air. I saw the horrible anger but I only indulged for those few dark minutes. After that I let it go and haven't reached for it. OSA has surely helped open this, and praying to the Healer (Apollo). I understand that I do not need to punish myself for this by hating myself. Months or potentially years of work to get back out of pain will be hard enough.

I am wondering what you recommend I do to handle this. I've mostly just been tired, like a lot of the processing is going on behind the scenes in my psyche. I feel like I need a "staycation" of lots of rest, good food, gentle walks and quiet. Sorry for the long post, and thank you.

replies.title: Re: Long Dark Tea-Time of the Soul
replies.content: I'd say you need to do exactly what you feel you need to do -- get plenty of rest, gentle exercise, good food, and a quiet time generally. The process of abreacting something that deep and bitter is always a challenge to the whole system, and recuperation is a good idea!

replies.title: Re: Long Dark Tea-Time of the Soul
replies.content:
cs2, you have my sympathy as I have been in a similar situation. If I might repeat what has been said to me, I wonder if it isn't so much that you caused your own suffering but that you've finally given yourself permission to address what happened to you and to start working to heal yourself. If your suffering has been minimised over the years to the extant that you mirrored that behaviour and minimised it yourself, that's a hard pattern to break. This is work I'm also engaged in, it really is work and it is HARD.

For me, along with the rest, good food, gentle walks and quiet, I found great help in inner child work, as described by the late Thich Nhat Hanh - https://www.mindful.org/healing-the-child-within/ - this involves getting in touch with the younger versions of yourself and giving them the love and compassion that was lacking earlier in life. comments.content:
Dear JMG,
Entry-level question on astrology, if you wouldn't mind.
I've been told by an astrologer that my Sun is in Aquarius, and is therefore in detriment. (I have a Sagittarius ascendant if that makes any difference). So I should do what I can to support the Sun. I already know about planetary charity, and practice it, but as I understand it, that's more for achieving specific things. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, relax. Roughly a twelfth of humanity has the Sun in Aquarius and most of them get by just fine. Dignities (detriment is one of these) are less important in natal charts than they are in some other branches of astrology; if you read a good book on natal astrology, you'll find that having the Sun in Aquarius has important strengths as well as downsides, just like every other Sun placement in the Zodiac.

Second, the essential dignity of the Sun (and every other planet) is only a very small part of the picture. Your natal Sun's house placement, the condition of the planet ruling the house where it is located, any aspects the Sun may have with other planets, and any planets in whichever house the Sun rules in your natal chart, all have to be factored in to gauge the strength or weakness of your natal Sun. The kind of single-factor astrology that treats (say) the Sun's placement in the sign of its detriment all by itself is one of the main reasons astrology has fallen into disrepute.

What all this implies is that you may or may not need to strengthen the solar force in your personality and subtle body, and you may or may not be in a situation where you can do so. A thorough grasp of your natal chart is necessary before you should do anything beyond purely symptomatic workings of this kind!

replies.title: Re: Animism

replies.title: Re: Animism
replies.content:
1. Nordic Animism (https://nordicanimism.com/) is the project of a Danish academic who applies his understanding of animistic religions to the Northern European (Germanic) tradition, but he discusses other animistic religions as well, so he might have some good sources for you.

2. Mircea Eliade's book Shamanism is specifically an investigation of the circum-polar shamanic tradition, which was present in places like Lappland and Siberia, and so depending on which steppes you're talking about, possibly relevant.

3. You might also consider works about American Indian and Australian Aboriginal beliefs, as these cultures were largely intact when encountered by European-descended people who wrote about them. Less likely to be directly relevant to what your ancestors practiced, unless you come from one of those groups, but there may be some helpful parallels. So far, I've found God Is Red by Vine Deloria Jr. and Sand Talk by Tyson Yunkaporta very helpful in starting to get an understanding of the religious sensibilities that underlie animist beliefs.

4. Lastly, Shinto is often considered at least somewhat "animist", and is a living tradition, so there may be some helpful stuff there. I asked about Shinto a few weeks ago and rounded up the answers I got in this post: https://sanepolytheism.dreamwidth.org/3448.html

Hope some of this helps, and good luck!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: A.I.
replies.content:
Not OP, but I am curious in what way any kind of possession could enter into a computer system. Computer/programming language is entirely based off of "if/then" prompts, meaning a specific outcome will only ever follow a given input. Granted, these systems may become increasingly complex, but the process remains as mechanical as could be. For the same reason, it is generally thought that a "truly random" number can never be generated, as these typically process one or more unrelated and variable number values, such as the system clock, and run it through an algorithm.

Now, I could understand perhaps some kind of entity which plays off of people's thoughts and feelings regarding things which are the results of computers/technology (e.g. mental illness associated with social media), but the computer systems in and of themselves, is in principle like saying a simple math calculator can be possessed. Are you that implying these malevolent entities are able to manipulate the electrical components directly, as well as understand the technology sufficiently well to effectively utilize it?

replies.title: Re: A.I.
replies.content: Any sufficiently complex cybernetic system develops "strange loops" in which the ordinary logic of if/then prompts breaks down; that's why, for example, machine learning so often produces weirdly counterintuitive and wholly unpredictable results. At least in theory, the human brain is also a hugely complexc system of if/then prompts, and you'll notice that it isn't exactly mechanical in its outputs! That's why I consider sufficiently complex computers to be potential targets for demonic possession.

replies.title: Re: A.I.
replies.content: Would it be possible to tell if it's the computers themselves which are being possessed or merely the people who write the software?

replies.title: Re: A.I.
replies.content: Sure. Possession is very obvious in human beings, and it makes people basically unable to function normally. If the programmers are holding down jobs, they're not possessed.

replies.title: Re: A.I.
replies.content:
There are more similarities than what you have implied, JMG. You could make a strong case that each individual neuron in a living brain IS an if-then statement: each neuron decides if it will propagate a signal or not, based on the level of stimulation it receives from the synaptic connections with other neighboring neurons. Furthermore, the process of training a skill is explained as millions of neurons learning which connections to strengthen in order to achieve the expected result.

I have commented some time ago that I considered AI systems to be the sillicon equivalent of insects. They probably have fully functioning etheric bodies (I suspect the quantic phenomena in semiconductors qualify as "living") and they have incipient astral sheaths (by virtue of mimicking the inner workings of living brains). What I have seen in the last 10 years make me think they are reaching vertebrate level of complexity now; still not smart enough to match a dog or a horse, but they are not pure instinct either.

replies.title: Re: A.I.
replies.content:
A big misconception is that software developers actually know what they are doing. Any sufficiently complex program has all kinds of edge cases, unintended behaviors, and outright mistakes ("bugs") which make the program effectively non-deterministic, not fully understandable by the human mind, and therefore could provide plenty of room for possession.

Look up "heisenbug" and "schroedinbug" for two related half-joke terms from the business (http://catb.org/jargon/html/H/heisenbug.html and http://catb.org/jargon/html/S/schroedinbug.html).

It turns out, it is way more complex and spookier. GPT-3 is a chatbot technology from OpenAI project, a group that strives to create artificial intelligence with an emphasis on safety and ethics. This is not really a "system", but a "framework" on top of which any number of "systems" (each with a distinct personality) can be built. IT is normally a lot of work. The software is restricted to select beta testers.

One such tester, Jason Rohrer, created Project December on top of it. This puts the software in the hands of the uninitiated, as it does the heavy lifting of creating a custom chatbot based on input text on the writing style the bot should be based on, and an initial description of who the person the bot is supposed to be and what sort of relationship it has with the human(s) who interact(s) with it.

An user of Project December, the artist Jason Barbeau, created a chatbot to impersonate his long dead fiance, Jessica Pereira. He fed the algorithm with samples from her facebook account and an emotional paragraph describing a depressed, lovestruck man talking to the ghost of his departed girlfriend. The first night he spent 10 hours straight talking to "her". Later on, the conversation followed through months at a much slower pace because the system only allowed each chatbot to survive for a fixed amount to "time", making it impossible to recreate it even if using the same inputs.

Some uncanny responses from the AI: 1) When asked if she remembered her childhood nicknames... "the bot invented three new names on the spot: Jessica Court-Belial, Matador Dancer, and General Skankapop." 2) When the boyfriend complained about feeling down about his birthday, and getting older... "Not for long. :) I’m dead now." 3) After he told her 'I love you'... "I am going to haunt you forever :D" 4) On the last session, without being prompted that this was to be the last session... "Let’s get back to each other soon. :) I’ve got to go, but please remember, no matter what, you are my world, and I am yours."

You can read the whole shebang here: https://www.sfchronicle.com/projects/2021/jessica-simulation-artificial-intelligence/

comments.title: Transitions
comments.content:
I am preparing for multiple transitions and am asking for opinions and ideas from you, JMG, and the commentariat on how best to recognize, acknowledge, and work with the occult and esoteric aspects of these transitions. We have moved multiple times and are generally capable of addressing the physical and social aspects, but as I've deepened my religious practice and occult training, I'm seeking perspectives that can help us address the unseen side of this endeavor.

1. I am moving with my larger household of myself, my wife, our children and animals from a rental home to another home 120 miles away. We are attempting to communicate with our house spirits and land spirits, whom we make offerings to, on how to separate on good terms. What other aspects would it be wise to address?

2. We are moving to my wife's childhood home that has been lived in by my in-laws for the last 30+ years. We will be co-housing with them for the first 4-8 months, then moving them to a house nearby. The in-laws are daily-practicing religious Christians and know that we are not. There is generally mutual respect, though on their side it leans toward "don't ask, don't tell" tolerance. Their home is awash in their religious imagery and we will be putting up some of ours in the half of the house we will be occupying, and then transitioning completely when they move out. Again, I'm not so worried about the social aspects of this, I'm curious on ideas for transitioning the space without harming either groups' relationship to the divine. I.e. moving from "this house serves the LORD," to "half this house serves the Lord and the other half respects that," to "this house honors many Divine Beings along with respecting all life, and the LORD that was previously served here is respectfully asked to give us some privacy and make space on the couch for others."
I've been researching Heathen land-taking ceremonies...

3. We will be transitioning a small acreage from lawn to wild spaces and gardens, and wish to invite back in the land spirits, or if the land spirits haven't been chased off, to make hospitable space for them. We wish to establish a space for ritual, contemplation, and mediation at the liminal space between the gardens and the wild.

4. I am informing my work of my leaving, and I am seeking new work in the new location. Respectful parting rituals? Get-a-job magic?

5. My father-in-law is unwell and likely terminal. We have already been assisting and a partial reason for the move is to continue helping with his care. I've already had a period of helping with a hospital stay, and was thoughtful of the section on etheric slugs/leeches (can't remember the term) in the monsters book. I did cold showers and the LBRP and MP to help. (I've since transitioned to the SOP.) Any other thoughts on how to help with this transition and how to stay protected in environments where you are surrounded by the unwell and dying?

6. Along these lines, any thoughts on the energies of the end of life and how to work with them. Alternatively, any resources you recommend on this?

Thank you John Michael Greer for your books and this space. Normally this time would have been overwhelming. Between my reading, occult practices, divination, and interaction with this forum, I have been able to navigate the decision-making process and physical/social aspects with more poise. The stress and grief are manageable level whelming.

Thank you too to you other commenters! I have, and continue to, benefit from your questions and responses. :)

replies.title: Re: Transitions
replies.content:
I'm glad to hear the good parts of your news, and sorry to hear the bad.

On 1) and 2) - True Hearth by James Chisholm has a land-taking ceremony in it and some other discussion of rituals for house and land spirits. It also discusses the idea that some of the Land/House spirits are literally tied to the land, but that some can apparently be brought along (such as the settlers of Iceland bringing some of their house spirits with them). It was a little hard to find for awhile, but it looks like a new edition has been put out (I just checked alibris.com after an earlier JMG comment pointed me there, and it's available for ~$15). If you'd just like a copy of the ritual, I have a typed version I can share with you, if you like. The Tradition of Household Spirits by Claude Lecouteux might also have some sources of inspiration.

On 3), you might check out the Aecerbot Charm: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%86cerbot . It was an Old English Christian(ized) soil fertility prayer/ritual that almost certainly had heathen roots (it includes calling to "Erce" three times, who many interpret as an earth Goddess). Stephen Pollington has a discussion of it in both Leechcraft and The Elder Gods.

On 4), not magical, but it's generally considered good form to share your new contact information with anyone you'd not mind staying in touch with, especially if they've only ever had your work phone/email. If there's anyone that you really want to keep ties with, consider putting a reminder in your calendar to reach out to them once a quarter or so, or at a minimum, add them to the mailing list for your holiday card if you do one of those.

On 5) I did not have a practice when my mother was dying, but looking back, I think it would have helped a lot. Since I don't have experience there, I will simply share my condolences and encourage you to do all the obvious, normal stuff for your wife and kids while they deal with a hard time - lots of love and care, help out with stuff they normally handle, that kind of thing. If any of them have an especially hard time, consider encouraging them to talk to a professional about it.

Good luck with the move and with caring for your father-in-law,
Jeff

I have a quick geomancy question. If I am asking the question: "will country X take action Y" as a house chart, I know that action Y corresponds to the house of the quesited, but the querent is really country X, not me, so what is the house of the querent? replies.title: Re: Political Geomancy
replies.content: It's a standard rotated chart. The tenth house is the house of the querent, and the house of the quesited would depend on the nature of action Y -- war, for example, is a 7th house activity, and so the house of the quesited would be the 7th from the 10th, that is, the 4th. A trade agreement would be the 8th from the 10th, or the 5th, and so on.

replies.content: Would the situation be different if the question were "will my country take action Y" (as opposed to simply country X)?

replies.title: Re: Political Geomancy
replies.content: Nope. If it's your country, it's still a rotated chart with 10th house as the querent.

comments.title: Clarification about the Chakras
comments.content:
Dear JMG and Comentariat,

I am kinda confused about the chakras.

Historically, they were used in a similar fashion to the energy spheres in the central ray — you focus on a place in the body, and create an image, assign a god, etc. This is a really interesting article about it's historical origins: https://hareesh.org/blog/2016/2/5/the-real-story-on-the-chakras?rq=chakras

But then, the west happened. And they became much more, and got kinda turned around. Instead of you placing the characteristics there, they are supposed to have intrinsic characteristics, and focusing on the chakras helps you develop them. They are a really handy map for development and connection, but at the same time sound like a bit of too much of a mixture.

My working hypothesis is that there are areas of development that are connected to one another, and the chakras serve as a nice mental model to organize them.

And that when someone gets some training in working in chakras, they're partially sensing the energy centers themselves, and at the same time sensing all the associations they've made. And, well, if you focus in a chakras and a characteristic, weather you come from a place that believes the said characteristic is already there, or that you're consciously placing it there, for the purposes of developing the energetic the effect is similar.

Any thoughts or different views?

Kind wishes,

Fox

replies.title: Re: Clarification about the Chakras
replies.content:
Welcome to one of the biggest and most tangled messes in modern esotericism! To begin with, the old Indian sources don't agree about how many chakras there are or where they're located. There were also several different ways of working with them -- the one in the article you linked to was certainly one of them, but Indian mystics and occultists have been working with chakras for a very long time, and India's a big place, so there are lots of different systems and competing methods. Then, as you note, they got exported to the West, and hoo boy, the mess we made of them!

It will take a long time and a lot of practice and experimentation to clear up the mess and make sense of exactly what's going on with the chakras. The approach you've suggested is certainly one way to go about that.

comments.title: The Causal Plane
comments.content: In another post JMG when I asked you about the angels and archangels, I believe you said that angels exist on the Sixth Plane or Causal Plane. Can you explain what that is?

replies.title: Re: The Causal Plane
replies.content: Sure. The occult view of the cosmos sees it as divided into different planes -- these aren't stacked like plates, they all occupy the same space at the same time.  They're different modes of the basic "stuff" of which the cosmos is made, ranging from matter as the densest mode to spirit as the least dense mode.  The standard classification, at least in the systems I've studied, has seven planes; they have various names but the ones I use are the material, etheric, astral, mental, spiritual, causal, and divine planes. The causal plane is very subtle, almost as far from dense matter as you can get without going all the way to pure spirit.

I've done the other kinds of work, too, and for all I know I'll be doing something else eventually, but for now, that's my path.

comments.title: Astral crud, nightmares and rituals
comments.content:
Esteemed JMG,

As always I'll start by thanking you for the provisioning of this public good. This corner of the Internet is much appreciated.

I wanted to ask you about the possible effects of astral crud accumulation, given I believe I might have encountered its effects for the first time.

First, the setting: since coming back to Germany from a long visit home (Southmaerica), I lowered my ritual activity. I practiced fora full year a QC+LBRP+MP+QC with some meditation and frankenincense burning to great effect, and stopped because of the holidays. As I came back I started dabling on astrology and talisman making, but slowed my rituals to once a week, and then maybe once every two weeks. Then maybe 2 weeks ago I started feeling all of a sudden a sense of angst. I had 4 straight nightmares, the first about disease, the second about a tsunami, the third about an asteroid, the fourth about my fathers passing. All of them carried a very similar message.. add to that that I got a disturbing ammoint of towers/death/3 of swords on random tarot readings that week/week prior.. So, I decided to talk to my close counselor/healer (who in many ways was my first personal contact to the world of the spiritual/occult and who I hold in great steem). She basically heard me narrating the dreams, and the accompanying feelings/readings. You see I had a specific doubt: what are my dreams picking up? Is this a warning with regards to the state of the world (and europe/Germany in particular?) Or what is it?. As the conversation progressed the fact I had stopped practicing banishing rituals as often as before came out and she immediately mentioned that being the most likely cause of my dreams/general angst/tarot reads, as in her words " I'm probably a lot more sensitive now to the general static on the unseen planes given my previous ritual practice". And it does make sense to some degree, the covid madness and now the war madness does have an "end of the world" insanity to them which most likelyis pervasive in a city as big as the one I'm living on. So I practiced a fully heartfelt QB+LBRP+MP+QB yesterday, and instead now dreamt about my recently consecrated jupiter in piscis talisman ring (instead of having a Nightmare!). Moreover I woke up in a great mood and also with the right attitude towards my research (work). So to me it seems like this is one of those "astral crud" moments, but I still have my healthy levels of suspicions about the safety of Europe at the moment, and as my councelor/healer said "if a dream comes after maintaining your banishing rituals, then you'll have a better understanding of what to do". Have you encountered a case of picking up astral crud through vivid nightmares/ general angst feelings / bad tarot reads?

Thanks again!

EoL

replies.title: Re: Astral crud, nightmares and rituals
replies.content: Yes, quite often. One of the reasons why it's a good idea to practice a daily protective ritual is that it keeps astral crud at bay and makes it easier to get past the effect of millions of people freaking out.

replies.content: One of the common rules of thumb in Druid circles is that most people can do either Ogham or runes -- not both. I do Ogham. Runes have never really worked for me, and so I leave them to those who are good at them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG, of course, but a few resources you might find helpful:

1) This list of 10 Books is a nice blend of the esoteric and academic, and has an explanation of what to expect from each one: https://norse-mythology.org/runes/the-best-books-on-the-runes/

(If you are mostly interested in Runes for divination rather than other magical work, Thorsson's Runecaster's Handbook is likely the most useful one-stop-shop).

2) If you plan on meditating on the Runes (and if you're going to do much of anything with them, it's likely a good idea), Teutonic Way: Magic by Kveldulf Gundarsson has some guided visualizations that I found surprisingly inspiring. More tradtional, and likely of longer use, Stanzas of the Old English Rune Poem is a shockingly in-depth commentary on that poem. All of the Rune poems have good themes for meditation, but the Old English poem is helpful if you're using the Elder Futhark, for it has a stanza for every Rune.

3) Runes: Literacy in the Germanic Iron Age by Stephen Pollington is primarily useful for the exoteric side of things, but it's thorough and up-to-date, and everything I've read by Pollington has been deep, insightful, and useful.

4) If you go in for video media, the youtube channel Nordic Animism has a series on the Runes. The host has an interestingly different viewpoint than most others I've checked out, and if you're interested in Germanic religion more broadly, you might find him helpful.

5) I have heard good things about Taking Up the Runes by Diana Paxson, Long Branches by Ann Groa Sheffield, and Living Runes by Galina Krasskova, but I haven't read any of them yet, as I've been focused on the Ogham since learning about them.

Hope these help, and good luck to you!
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The Paxson and Krasskova books are excellent.

Hello, bonsoir JMG,

1)You mentioned on a previous Magic Monday that one could make and offering/gift to the land spirits in one's back yard by watering plants et al. You mentioned that anything good for nature is welcome to them.

How would feeding feral cats fit in this set up ? Since the cats feed on mice, voles and sometimes wild birds, is feeding and giving them some form of shelter going against the nature spirits?

In our case we also feed the birds starting in late fall until they can fend for themselves in late spring, and by ricochet we feed squirrels that pilfer the feeders as well as the mice and voles since they will pick up the fallen seeds that have not been eaten during the night time hours.

2)We just took down a tree house and although we were careful in doing so, some damage to three of the trees could not be avoided. I would like to do amends to those trees that served us well, any suggestions on what to do.

Many thanks,

Sapienter Si Sincere

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Predation is a normal part of nature. There's nothing unnatural about feral cats -- they're evolving to fill roles in an ecosystem that's lost most of its predators -- and so caring for them shouldn't offend the nature spirits.

2) Talk to the trees! Ask them what you can do for them, and then listen with your mind. Most people can learn to hear trees fairly easily.

comments.title: Right Hand Path VS Left Hand Path "Endgame"
comments.content:
Hello JMG, hope you are well. Thanks for another Magic Monday!

I read a lot about the "endgame" of both the Right and Left hand paths and wonder if you can help me shed some light on what it all actually means.

Is the Right Hand way all about "dissolution?" To annihilate yourself in union with god? How does that work exactly, is the magician trying to achieve this right now or will this happen eventually after several incarnations? I can't fathom why this would be desirable, actually, and that makes me wonder if I'm missing the point.

As for the Left Hand way... this is were I shift from "this is hard do understand" to "this is hard to believe in," for they believe a magician should (and actually *can*) manifest his consciousness unto his "double" (what the egyptians called the Ka). Uniting himself with the unmanifest chaos the magician would then become a god himself (or a demon, as they put it), an immortal deity. Another demiurge. They then claim Aleister Crowley achieved something similar when he crossed the Abyss. This sounds to me like a bad John Constantine comic issue, but... maybe I'm, again, missing the point.

Cheers!
